title: Sudden Nosebleed From Both Nostrils in Dogs: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog's nose bleeding from both nostrils? Learn why bilateral epistaxis is a high-priority emergency and what you should do immediately.
content_html:
What is Bilateral Epistaxis?

Sudden bleeding from both nostrils, medically termed bilateral epistaxis, is a concerning clinical sign in dogs. Unlike a single-sided bleed which may be caused by a local irritant, bleeding from both sides often indicates a systemic issue where the body's ability to clot blood has been compromised or there is widespread inflammation or vascular damage.

Is it an Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for sudden bleeding from both nostrils is High. Because this often points to internal issues such as toxin ingestion (like rat poison), blood clotting disorders, or severe systemic infections, you should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately. It is vital to monitor for secondary signs of shock, such as pale gums, rapid breathing, or collapse.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you can safely do so, taking a clear photo of the bleeding and any discharge can be incredibly helpful for the veterinary team. A photo captures the volume, color, and consistency of the blood at its peak, which may change by the time you reach the clinic. This helps the triage nurse quickly determine the severity and may provide clues to the underlying cause, allowing for faster life-saving treatment.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of bleeding from both nostrils?
faqs.answer: Common causes include rodenticide (rat poison) ingestion, clotting disorders like Von Willebrand disease, severe high blood pressure, or acute systemic infections.

faqs.question: How can I help stop the bleeding on the way to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as calm and still as possible. You may gently apply a cold compress to the bridge of the nose, but avoid sticking anything into the nostrils or tilting the head back.

faqs.question: What will the vet do first?
faqs.answer: The vet will prioritize stabilizing your dog, checking gum color and heart rate, and likely performing blood tests to check clotting times and platelet counts.
